past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, well controlled on medication
Coronary artery disease with prior percutaneous coronary intervention 5 years ago
Peripheral arterial disease with intermittent claudication
Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (IgA), diagnosed 2 years ago, under hematologic surveillance
Mild distal sensory neuropathy, diagnosed 3 years ago

medications.indication: Recently started for presumed polymyalgia rheumatica

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 68
Mother had hypertension and dementia in her 80s
No family history of hematological malignancies or autoimmune disease

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago, 30 pack-year history
social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinking, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in single-story home
social_history.travel: No recent travel outside the country

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but appears acutely ill and uncomfortable. Oriented to person and place but slow to give date. GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6). Mild inattention on serial 7s.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive. Photophobia present with discomfort on light exposure. Fundoscopic exam shows no papilledema. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and motor function intact. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Proximal upper extremity weakness rated 4/5 bilaterally with tenderness to palpation of deltoids and biceps. Distal strength 5/5 in upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased vibration sense at both great toes and medial malleoli. Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing with mild action tremor bilaterally but no dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Not assessed due to acute illness and patient debility.
neurological_exam.additional: Marked nuchal rigidity present. Positive Kernig sign bilaterally. Positive Brudzinski sign. Jolt accentuation of headache present.

patient.chief_complaint: High fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness developing over the past 48 hours in a 74-year-old man recently started on corticosteroids.
patient.history_present_illness: A 74-year-old man with a history of hypertension, coronary artery disease, peripheral arterial disease, and monoclonal IgA gammopathy of undetermined significance presents to the emergency department with acute onset of high fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. For the preceding three months prior to this presentation, he had experienced fluctuating headaches, fatigue, proximal muscle weakness in his upper extremities, low-grade fevers up to 38.3 degrees Celsius, unintentional weight loss of approximately 7 kilograms, and diffuse joint pain with temporal tenderness. He was evaluated extensively as an outpatient including inflammatory markers, serum protein electrophoresis, blood cultures, echocardiography, and cross-sectional imaging of the chest and abdomen, which were largely unremarkable aside from an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and the known IgA monoclonal gammopathy. A presumptive diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica was made, and he was started on prednisolone 30 mg daily approximately two weeks ago, with initial improvement in his musculoskeletal symptoms. Ten days after starting prednisolone, he developed rapid-onset exertional exhaustion and returned to his primary care physician, who noted persistent low-grade fevers and referred him to the emergency department. Over the subsequent 48 hours, his condition deteriorated markedly with the development of high spiking fevers to 39 degrees Celsius, a progressively worsening generalized headache described as the worst of his life, photophobia, nausea with two episodes of vomiting, and pronounced neck stiffness. On arrival to the emergency department, he is febrile, tachycardic, and appears acutely toxic. He is alert but confused regarding the date. His neck is markedly rigid with positive meningeal signs. There is no rash.

findings.location: Diffuse, bilateral cerebral convexities and basal cisterns

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle sulcal hyperintensity in bilateral frontoparietal regions

findings.location: Scattered cortical sulci bilaterally, predominantly frontoparietal

signal_characteristics.DWI: High signal intensity within cortical sulci consistent with purulent exudate
signal_characteristics.ADC: Low ADC values within affected sulci

additional_observations: No subdural empyema or epidural abscess identified
No cerebral vein thrombosis on MR venography sequences
No evidence of hydrocephalus; ventricular system is normal in size
Mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes in periventricular white matter
mri.impression: Small chronic left cerebellar lacunar infarct, likely incidental. Age-appropriate cerebral atrophy and mild chronic microvascular ischemic changes. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in leads V4-V6
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia, likely secondary to fever and systemic infection. Nonspecific ST-T wave changes that may be rate-related. No acute ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 22.4 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 89 % (H), Band forms 12 % (H), Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL (L), Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), CO2 21 mEq/L (L), BUN 24 mg/dL (H), Glucose 168 mg/dL (H), CRP 248 mg/L (H), ESR 92 mm/h (H), Procalcitonin 8.6 ng/mL (H), PT 14.2 seconds (H), INR 1.2  (H), D-dimer 3.8 mg/L FEU (H), Fibrinogen 485 mg/dL (H), Aerobic bottle Gram-positive diplococci identified at 8 hours  (H), Lactate 3.4 mmol/L (H).

special_tests.Intrathecal_IgA_synthesis: Elevated (intrathecal IgA > IgG), noted but likely related to known serum IgA gammopathy

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 32 cmH2O. WBC: 2840 cells/uL (94% PMN/4% lymph). Protein: 3120 mg/L (312 mg/dL). Glucose: 12 mg/dL (ratio 0.07 (serum glucose 168 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: Gram-positive diplococci seen, moderate quantity. CSF_lactate: 11.2 mmol/L (markedly elevated, reference <3.5). Latex_agglutination: Positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen. HSV_PCR: Negative. VZV_PCR: Negative. Enterovirus_PCR: Negative. Cryptococcal_antigen: Negative. Intrathecal_IgA_synthesis: Elevated (intrathecal IgA > IgG), noted but likely related to known serum IgA gammopathy. AFB_smear: Negative.

output.opening_pressure: 24 cmH2O (still elevated but trending down from 32)

special_tests.CSF_culture: Streptococcus pneumoniae confirmed, sensitive to penicillin (MIC 0.02), ceftriaxone (MIC <0.06), vancomycin (MIC 0.5)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 24 cmH2O. WBC: 680 cells/uL (down from 2840) (72% PMN/22% lymph). Protein: 1840 mg/L (184 mg/dL, down from 312). Glucose: 34 mg/dL (improving from 12) (ratio 0.24 (serum glucose 142 mg/dL)). CSF_culture: Streptococcus pneumoniae confirmed, sensitive to penicillin (MIC 0.02), ceftriaxone (MIC <0.06), vancomycin (MIC 0.5). CSF_lactate: 6.8 mmol/L (down from 11.2, trending toward normal). Gram_stain: No organisms seen on repeat.

additional_observations: No space-occupying lesion that would contraindicate lumbar puncture
output.impression: CT head without contrast shows no acute intracranial pathology. No mass lesion, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus. Incidental chronic left cerebellar lacunar infarct. Age-appropriate atrophy. No contraindication to lumbar puncture. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Aerobic bottle - final identification Streptococcus pneumoniae (serotype 3)  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Blood culture positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae serotype 3
Streptococcus pneumoniae DETECTED on CSF PCR panel

output.impression: Abnormal values: Right ear pure tone average (PTA) 35 dB HL dB HL (H), Left ear pure tone average (PTA) 40 dB HL dB HL (H), Speech discrimination - Right 84% % (H), Speech discrimination - Left 76% % (H).

results.title: Adjunctive Dexamethasone in Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: de Gans J, van de Beek D
results.summary: Landmark RCT demonstrating that adjunctive dexamethasone (10 mg IV q6h x4 days) given before or with the first dose of antibiotics reduces unfavorable outcomes and mortality in adults with pneumococcal meningitis.

results.title: Practice Guidelines for Bacterial Meningitis - IDSA 2004
results.summary: IDSA guidelines recommend empiric ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin (if Listeria risk) for adults >50 years. Adjunctive dexamethasone recommended for suspected pneumococcal meningitis. Duration of therapy for pneumococcal meningitis: 10-14 days.

results.title: Bacterial Meningitis in Immunocompromised Patients
results.summary: Immunocompromised patients (including those on corticosteroids) are at increased risk for bacterial meningitis and atypical organisms including Listeria. Higher mortality in this population. Empiric coverage must include ampicillin for Listeria in addition to standard ceftriaxone + vancomycin.

output.summary: In immunocompromised patients on corticosteroids, bacterial meningitis requires broad empiric coverage including ampicillin for Listeria in addition to ceftriaxone and vancomycin. Adjunctive dexamethasone has proven benefit for pneumococcal meningitis. Once pneumococcus is confirmed and susceptibilities known, therapy can be narrowed. Duration of treatment is typically 10-14 days for pneumococcal meningitis.

output.proposed: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + Vancomycin 1g IV q12h + Dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h
interactions: Ceftriaxone may cause false-positive urine glucose tests in patients on glucose monitoring
Vancomycin and ceftriaxone: no significant pharmacokinetic interaction, but combination increases nephrotoxicity risk - monitor renal function
warnings: Patient is already on prednisolone 30 mg daily - consider transitioning to dexamethasone for adjunctive meningitis treatment and discontinuing prednisolone to avoid excessive immunosuppression
Monitor renal function closely with vancomycin dosing - obtain vancomycin trough levels
Aspirin continuation: assess bleeding risk given potential coagulopathy in sepsis
alternatives: If penicillin-susceptible pneumococcus confirmed: may narrow to ceftriaxone monotherapy or high-dose IV penicillin G
Meropenem as alternative if cephalosporin allergy

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 6-7 Hz theta activity bilaterally, no well-formed alpha rhythm

findings.frequency: Predominantly theta (5-7 Hz) with intermittent delta (2-3 Hz)

artifacts.impact: Mild, does not significantly obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Not performed due to clinical condition
activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to clinical condition

output.impression: No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures identified. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Recording duration 30 minutes. Activating procedures not performed.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae

differential.key_distinguishing: Subacute course over weeks, basilar predominance on MRI, lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis, very low glucose but less abrupt onset. The elevated intrathecal IgA is a potential confounder but explained by known monoclonal gammopathy.

Immunosuppressed host on corticosteroids raises concern, but CSF shows neutrophilic predominance (not lymphocytic), and cryptococcal antigen is negative. Fungal meningitis typically has more insidious course.

Viral meningitis typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal-to-mildly elevated protein, normal glucose. This CSF profile with very high WBC, high protein, very low glucose, and positive Gram stain excludes viral etiology.
Known MGUS could raise concern for myeloma-related meningeal disease, but acute febrile presentation with positive Gram stain and bacterial cultures is incompatible.

Sudden onset headache could overlap, but fever, nuchal rigidity with positive Kernig/Brudzinski signs, and turbid CSF with neutrophilic pleocytosis point to meningitis. No xanthochromia or elevated RBCs in CSF.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head to rule out mass lesion or raised intracranial pressure before lumbar puncture, given altered mental status in elderly patient
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No mass lesion, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus; safe to proceed with LP

optimal_actions.action: Draw blood cultures before initiating antibiotics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Blood cultures positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV antibiotics immediately (ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin for age >50) with adjunctive dexamethasone
Perform lumbar puncture and analyze CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, and culture
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Turbid CSF with neutrophilic pleocytosis, elevated protein, very low glucose, Gram-positive diplococci on stain

optimal_actions.action: Obtain complete laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers, coagulation, and serum lactate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, elevated CRP and procalcitonin, hyponatremia, hyperglycemia

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for complications of meningitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leptomeningeal enhancement with restricted diffusion in sulci consistent with purulent exudate

optimal_actions.action: Order CSF multiplex PCR panel (FilmArray) for rapid pathogen identification
Narrow antibiotic therapy once susceptibilities available and discontinue prednisolone
Check drug interactions for meningitis treatment regimen in context of current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Consider transitioning prednisolone to dexamethasone; monitor renal function with vancomycin

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on management of bacterial meningitis in immunocompromised patients
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Broader empiric coverage needed; must include Listeria coverage for elderly/immunosuppressed

critical_actions: Start empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin for Listeria coverage given age >50 and immunosuppression) within 1 hour of presentation
Administer adjunctive dexamethasone before or with first dose of antibiotics for suspected pneumococcal meningitis
Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for cell count, chemistry, Gram stain, culture, and PCR panel
Discontinue or taper prednisolone given it is an immunosuppressant contributing to infection risk
Monitor for complications: seizures, SIADH, hearing loss, cerebral edema
contraindicated_actions: Delaying antibiotics to obtain imaging or LP - antibiotics must not be delayed for any diagnostic workup
Performing LP without prior CT in a patient with altered mental status (risk of herniation)
Continuing prednisolone 30 mg daily without reassessment - immunosuppression likely facilitated invasive infection
Dismissing meningeal signs as musculoskeletal (polymyalgia rheumatica) without CSF analysis
Administering lumbar puncture if CT shows mass effect or obstructive hydrocephalus
key_reasoning_points: Elderly patient on immunosuppressive corticosteroids (prednisolone 30 mg/day) is at high risk for opportunistic and invasive bacterial infections including meningitis

The temporal sequence is critical: months of constitutional symptoms treated as polymyalgia rheumatica, then acute deterioration after starting corticosteroids, suggesting the steroid immunosuppression unmasked or facilitated an invasive infection
Classic triad of fever, neck stiffness, and altered mental status with positive meningeal signs (Kernig, Brudzinski) points strongly to meningitis

CSF profile is unambiguously bacterial: high neutrophilic pleocytosis (2840 WBC, 94% PMNs), very high protein (312 mg/dL), critically low glucose (12 mg/dL, ratio 0.07), positive Gram stain showing diplococci

The known monoclonal IgA gammopathy with elevated intrathecal IgA could be a red herring suggesting TB meningitis (where intrathecal IgA > IgG is typical), but in this case it reflects the serum gammopathy rather than a tuberculous process
Age >50 and immunosuppression mandate empiric Listeria coverage with ampicillin in addition to ceftriaxone and vancomycin
Adjunctive dexamethasone is evidence-based for pneumococcal meningitis (de Gans/van de Beek trial) and should be given before or with the first antibiotic dose
Blood cultures growing gram-positive diplococci and latex agglutination positive for S. pneumoniae antigen provide rapid etiologic confirmation before final culture results

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward presentation with classic meningeal signs, high fever, and unambiguous CSF findings. The immunosuppressive context (corticosteroids) adds clinical interest but does not obscure the diagnosis.
